8	---
9	# ADR-004: Use Bead-Backed Collections for DDx Runtime Storage
10	
11	## Status
12	
13	Accepted
14	
15	## Context
16	
17	DDx now has multiple local storage needs beyond the primary work queue:
18	
19	- active work items
20	- archived work items
21	- execution definitions and run indexes
22	- agent session indexes
23	- future runtime record families
24	
25	The current bead implementation already provides useful properties:
26	
27	- a portable bead schema with unknown-field preservation
28	- interchangeable local backends (`jsonl`, `bd`, `br`)
29	- locking and atomic rewrite behavior
30	
31	The current implementation is also too tightly coupled to one specific file,
32	`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, and to one primary use case, the active tracker.
33	
34	At the same time, execution runs and agent sessions can carry large payloads
35	such as prompts, responses, raw logs, and structured result blobs. Those
36	payloads should not be forced inline into one shared JSONL record.
37	
38	This decision must stay aligned with the bd/br interchange contract rather than
39	silently drifting into a DDx-only fork.
40	
41	Local compatibility anchors:
42	
43	- [types.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/types.go)
44	- [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go)
45	- [backend_external.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/backend_external.go)
46	- [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
47	- [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json)
48	
49	Upstream reference points:
50	
51	- `bd` upstream repository: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads>
52	- upstream format directory: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/tree/main/format>
53	- upstream Dolt storage notes: <https://github.com/gastownhall/beads/blob/main/docs/DOLT.md>
54	
55	DDx's external backend adapter currently treats both `bd` and `br` through the
56	same interchange surface:
57	
58	- `list --json` for reads
59	- `import --from jsonl --replace -` for writes
60	
61	That contract is the minimum compatibility surface this ADR must preserve.
62	
63	## Decision
64	
65	DDx will use the bead schema and backend model as the logical storage engine
66	for runtime records, but it will separate logical collections from one
67	specific physical file.
68	
69	1. DDx storage is organized as **named bead-backed collections**.
70	2. The primary tracker is one collection, not the only collection.
71	3. JSONL maps a collection to a separate file under `.ddx/`.
72	4. Other backends (`bd`, `br`) must preserve the same logical collection
73	   boundary, but their physical storage layout is a backend concern rather than
74	   a DDx file-layout concern.
75	5. Large payloads are stored in **sidecar attachment files** referenced by the
76	   bead-schema record, not inline in the collection row.
77	6. The base bead envelope must retain bd/br-compatible field names and
78	   semantics for the shared fields DDx already interoperates on.
79	
80	## Consequences
81	
82	### Positive
83	
84	- One reusable schema and backend family can serve work items, archives,
85	  execution indexes, and agent-session indexes.
86	- DDx can add new record families without inventing a new local storage model
87	  each time.
88	- Archived beads can move out of the active queue without changing the storage
89	  engine.
90	- Execution and agent records can keep lightweight searchable metadata in the
91	  bead collection while storing large bodies separately.
92	
93	### Negative
94	
95	- The current bead store API must be generalized from a fixed `beads.jsonl`
96	  target to a named collection abstraction.
97	- Collection-specific semantics must be documented clearly because not every
98	  bead-backed collection uses queue semantics such as `ready` or `blocked`.
99	- DDx must not assume that every backend exposes collections as separate files.
100	
101	## Collection Model
102	
103	Examples of logical collections:
104	
105	- `beads` — active work queue
106	- `beads-archive` — archived work items
107	- `exec-runs` — execution run metadata records
108	- `agent-sessions` — agent session metadata records
109	
110	Each record still uses the bead envelope:
111	
112	- `id`
113	- `issue_type`
114	- `status`
115	- `title`
116	- `labels`
117	- `created_at`
118	- `updated_at`
119	- preserved unknown fields for domain-specific data
120	
121	Shared field names remain aligned with bd/br interchange:
122	
123	- `issue_type`, not `type`
124	- `owner`, not `assignee`
125	- `created_at` / `updated_at`, not `created` / `updated`
126	- `dependencies`, not `deps`
127	
128	Collection-specific fields live in preserved extras. For example:
129	
130	- execution runs may store `definition_id`, `artifact_ids`, `run_status`,
131	  `provenance`, and attachment references
132	- agent sessions may store `harness`, `model`, `tokens`, `correlation`, and
133	  attachment references
134	
135	## Attachment Model
136	
137	Large bodies are stored outside the collection row.
138	
139	Examples:
140	
141	- prompt text
142	- response text
143	- stdout or stderr logs
144	- structured result JSON
145	
146	The bead-backed metadata record stores stable references to these payloads.
147	
148	Attachment references are DDx-specific extensions carried in preserved extras.
149	They must not rename or redefine the shared bead-envelope fields that bd/br
150	already understand.
151	
152	## Technical Guards
153	
154	This ADR is only acceptable if the compatibility contract is enforced in-repo.
155	
156	Required guards:
157	
158	1. The base bead envelope is defined in
159	   [bead-record.schema.json](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema/bead-record.schema.json).
160	2. Unknown fields remain allowed at the schema level so bd/br computed fields
161	   and DDx-specific extensions both round-trip.
162	3. [schema_compat_test.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/schema_compat_test.go)
163	   must keep locking the field names and round-trip behavior against real bd
164	   export examples.
165	4. [marshal.go](/home/erik/Projects/ddx/cli/internal/bead/marshal.go) remains
166	   the canonical field-mapping implementation for DDx bead JSON.
167	5. Collection-specific runtime records may add their own schemas, but those
168	   schemas must layer on top of the base bead record rather than fork it.
169	
170	If upstream bd/br changes the shared JSON field contract, DDx must update the
171	schema file, compatibility tests, and this ADR together.
172	
173	## Not Chosen
174	
175	### Separate bespoke storage formats per subsystem
176	
177	Rejected because it multiplies local persistence models and weakens backend
178	reuse.
179	
180	### Storing all execution and session payloads inline in collection rows
181	
182	Rejected because prompts, responses, and logs can grow large and would make
183	shared collection rewrites fragile and expensive.
184	
185	### Reusing only bead locking code while inventing a new record schema
186	
187	Rejected because the bead schema is already the portable DDx record envelope
188	and should remain the common logical model.
189

# ADR-026: Evidence Storage Architecture (3-Tier)

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2026-05-10
**Authors:** operator decision after evidence-storage cleanup discoveries

## 1. Context

DDx accumulates per-attempt execution evidence — `manifest.json`, `prompt.md`,
`result.json`, `reviewer-stream.log`, agent stream logs in `.ddx/agent-logs/`,
and worker state in `.ddx/workers/` — without a documented retention policy or
storage architecture.

The problem surfaced concretely:

- 12,096 files tracked in git under `.ddx/executions/` via un-ignore rules.
- 860 MB of agent logs accumulated locally under `.ddx/agent-logs/`, which was
  gitignored but unbounded on disk.
- No ADR or spec describes the storage tiers, retention policy, or the operator
  interface for archive.
- Implementation is inconsistent: `executions/` was tracked via un-ignore,
  `agent-logs/` gitignored, `workers/` not in gitignore but also not actively
  tracked.
- No central metrics extraction; cross-attempt queries require fanning out reads
  across unbounded local directories.

The architecture must enable optimization analysis (model / prompt / harness /
spec performance) WITHOUT forcing all detailed evidence into git.

Cross-references:

- TD-031 §4 — event vocabulary (event kinds written into `.ddx/attachments/`)
- SD-025 — task execution lifecycle and on-disk layout

## 2. Decision

DDx adopts a **3-tier evidence storage architecture**:

| Tier | Name | Location | Git-tracked |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Durable structured state | `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/attachments/`, `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` | Yes |
| 2 | Local ephemeral evidence | `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/agent-logs/`, `.ddx/workers/` | No |
| 3 | External archive | operator-configured mirror hook | No (operator choice) |

Tier 1 (git) is the shared truth for correctness questions: did the bead close,
what commit, which events fired. Tier 2 (local) is the per-machine detail
record for investigation: what was in the prompt, what did the model stream,
what did the reviewers say. Tier 3 (external) is an optional operator-configured
mirror for off-machine or long-term retention.

## 3. Principles

### P1 — Git holds durable, small, structured state

Git tracks only content that must survive machine loss and is small enough to
keep repository size manageable.

Git-tracked paths:

- `.ddx/beads.jsonl` — active bead records (per bead: ~2–5 KB)
- `.ddx/beads-archive.jsonl` — closed beads migrated out of the active tracker
- `.ddx/attachments/<bead-id>/events.jsonl` — lifecycle event log for each bead
  (~1–3 KB per bead, append-only per TD-031 §4)
- `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` — one row per bead-attempt; the queryable
  analytics table (see P4; ~300 bytes per attempt)
- Code, docs, ADRs, TDs, FEATs — all governed artifacts

**Rationale:** git provides history, replication, and forensics at zero
operational cost for small files. Adding large or ephemeral files to git
inflates clone size, slows history traversal, and pollutes the forensic record
with data that is primarily useful only on the originating machine.

### P2 — Local disk holds detailed, ephemeral, retention-bounded evidence

Per-machine evidence that is too large or too ephemeral for git lives under
`.ddx/` but is excluded from tracking via `.gitignore`.

Local-only paths:

- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` — full evidence bundle: `prompt.md`,
  `manifest.json`, `result.json`, `usage.json`, `checks.json`,
  `reviewer-stream.log`, `decisions.md`, and any other per-attempt artifacts
- `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` — raw model token stream logs
- `.ddx/workers/worker-*/` — per-worker runtime state and heartbeat files

**Default retention:** 90 days. Evidence older than the retention window is
eligible for removal by the DDx cleanup manager (SD-025 Cleanup Manager).

**Operator override:** the retention window is configurable in
`.ddx/config.yaml` as `evidence.retention_days`. Setting it to `0` disables
automatic removal (useful when an external archive mirror is running). The
cleanup manager respects the configured value at every inline, loop, and
background cleanup pass.

**Rationale:** detailed evidence (prompts, raw streams, reviewer logs) is
primarily useful for the session that produced it and for short-term
investigation. Keeping it per-machine avoids repo bloat while still making it
accessible for local debugging and analysis. The retention window balances
investigation utility against unbounded local disk growth.

### P3 — External archive is operator-configured, not built-in

DDx provides a **mirror hook** that fires after each evidence bundle is
finalized. The hook receives the attempt ID and evidence directory path as
arguments. The operator connects whatever backend they need.

Supported hook entry points (configurable in `.ddx/config.yaml`):

```yaml
evidence:
  mirror_hook: ".ddx/hooks/mirror-evidence.sh"
```

Default hook: none (mirror is opt-in).

Reference backends (not bundled in DDx, operator-maintained):

- **local-dir** — copy evidence bundles to a configured local directory
- **S3** (deferred) — upload to an S3-compatible bucket

**Rationale:** DDx serves diverse environments (air-gapped machines, shared
servers, personal laptops). A built-in backend mandate would impose operational
cost on operators who need different storage targets or no external archive at
all. The hook pattern keeps DDx core small while enabling any backend through a
shell script. Remote backends are deferred because the local-dir pattern covers
the immediate need.

### P4 — Aggregate metrics are denormalized, queryable, and durable

`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` is the analytics table. Each row represents one
bead-attempt and carries denormalized fields that enable cross-bead queries
without fan-out reads across ephemeral evidence directories.

Minimum row fields:

```json
{
  "attempt_id": "20260510T214111-a50a6b75",
  "bead_id": "ddx-7e9e5e1a",
  "started_at": "2026-05-10T21:41:11Z",
  "finished_at": "2026-05-10T21:55:00Z",
  "outcome": "closed_merged",
  "harness": "claude",
  "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
  "input_tokens": 12480,
  "output_tokens": 3210,
  "cost_usd": 0.042,
  "commit_sha": "abc123",
  "spec_ids": ["FEAT-022"],
  "labels": ["phase:6", "area:docs", "kind:doc"]
}
```

The row schema is extensible; future fields are added without breaking existing
readers. `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` is git-tracked so that analytics survive
machine loss and are shareable across team members on the same project.

**Rationale:** cross-attempt performance queries (which harness closes beads
fastest, which specs have the highest retry count, which models have the lowest
cost per close) require structured data. Fan-out reads across
`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` are too slow and fail when evidence has been
purged. A separate denormalized table written at finalization time gives
analytics durability without inflating the primary bead record or requiring
local ephemeral evidence to persist indefinitely.

## 4. Per-Bead Git Footprint

For a typical bead with three attempts before close:

| Path | Size |
|---|---|
| 1 row in `.ddx/beads.jsonl` | ~2–5 KB |
| 1 file `.ddx/attachments/<bead-id>/events.jsonl` | ~1–3 KB |
| 3 rows in `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` | ~900 bytes (~300 bytes each) |
| **Total tracked** | **~5–8 KB** |

Under the previous architecture (executions/ tracked via un-ignore), the same
bead accumulated ~95 KB of tracked content across three attempts. This
architecture reduces the per-bead git footprint by approximately 20x.

At scale (1,000 closed beads, average 3 attempts each), the tracked footprint
grows by roughly 5–8 MB. The same scale under the previous architecture would
have grown by ~95 MB.

## 5. Local Retention Default and Operator Override

**Default:** 90 days from the attempt finalization timestamp recorded in
`manifest.json`. Evidence finalized more than 90 days ago is eligible for
removal at the next cleanup pass.

**Override:** configure `evidence.retention_days` in `.ddx/config.yaml`:

```yaml
evidence:
  retention_days: 90       # default; set to 0 to disable automatic removal
```

Setting `retention_days: 0` disables automatic removal entirely. This is
appropriate when a mirror hook is archiving evidence to an external system and
the operator wants the hook to control deletion rather than the DDx cleanup
manager.

The cleanup manager (SD-025 Cleanup Manager) applies the configured retention
window during inline cleanup (`ddx try`), loop cleanup (`ddx work`), background
cleanup, and the explicit `ddx cleanup` operator command.

Retention applies to all three local paths:

- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`
- `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-*.jsonl` (by mtime, since these are not keyed by
  attempt)
- `.ddx/workers/worker-*/` (by liveness expiry; a live worker's state is never
  removed)

**Preserved evidence is exempt:** worktrees and evidence bundles registered as
preserved (SD-025 conservative deletion rules) are never subject to automatic
retention-based removal. Preserved evidence must be explicitly released by the
operator before the cleanup manager will remove it.

## 6. External Archive Interface

DDx exposes a single hook point for external evidence archival. The hook fires
after an evidence bundle is finalized and before the attempt record is closed.

Hook contract:

- **Environment variables passed to the hook:**
  - `DDX_ATTEMPT_ID` — the attempt identifier
  - `DDX_EVIDENCE_DIR` — absolute path to `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`
  - `DDX_BEAD_ID` — the bead being attempted
  - `DDX_OUTCOME` — the finalization outcome (e.g., `closed_merged`, `no_changes_verified`)
- **Exit code:** 0 means success; nonzero means the mirror failed. A nonzero
  exit does not abort the bead close — it is recorded in the attempt record as
  a mirror warning.
- **Timeout:** hooks must complete within 60 seconds. DDx kills the hook process
  after the timeout and records a mirror timeout warning.

No built-in backends are bundled. Operators write hook scripts. Example (local
directory mirror):

```bash
#!/bin/bash
cp -r "$DDX_EVIDENCE_DIR" "/mnt/evidence-archive/${DDX_BEAD_ID}/"
```

The hook interface is intentionally minimal. DDx does not define upload
protocols, credential management, or retry logic — those are operator
responsibilities in the hook script. A future `ddx mirror` subcommand may
provide a curated set of backends, but that is out of scope for this ADR.

## 7. Cross-Machine Considerations

### Single-machine assumption for `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl`

`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` is git-tracked and shared via the project
repository. However, rows are written by the machine executing the attempt, and
the file is append-only per-machine before commit.

**Single-machine assumption:** within one drain session, all attempts.jsonl
appends originate from one process on one machine. Concurrent multi-machine
draining against the same git repository is not a supported configuration today.
Attempts from different machines are merged through ordinary git merge — a
conflict means two machines appended rows simultaneously, which is a
misconfiguration under current topology.

**Forward pointer to federation:** when FEAT-026 (federation topology) lands,
`.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` will need to be partitioned by machine or node.
The anticipated partition key is `host` (already in the row schema as the actor
or executor identity). Federated reads will fan out across per-machine files
and merge on the client side. This ADR does not block federation; it explicitly
defers the partition design to the federation bead.

### `.ddx/executions/` is per-machine working state

Per the execution evidence convention in `CLAUDE.md`: `.ddx/executions/` is
per-machine working state — it is listed in `.gitignore` and is never committed.
The durable audit trail is the events stream in `.ddx/attachments/`, not the
execution directories.

No cross-machine evidence sharing is expected for local tier-2 data. Operators
who need cross-machine evidence access should configure a tier-3 external archive
(section 6).

## 8. Migration Plan

This ADR describes the target architecture. The following sister beads implement
the transition:

| Bead | Work |
|---|---|
| B29 — retention enforcement | Implement the 90-day default retention sweep in the DDx cleanup manager (SD-025). Add `evidence.retention_days` config key. Add `TestRetentionSweep_*` tests. |
| B30 — gitignore hardening | Audit and lock down `.gitignore` entries for all local-only paths: `executions/`, `agent-logs/`, `workers/`. Remove any remaining un-ignore rules that leaked executions into git. Add CI check that no `.ddx/executions/` files are staged. |
| B31 — mirror hook interface | Implement the `evidence.mirror_hook` config key, the hook invocation at finalization, the timeout enforcer, and the warning record on nonzero exit. Add `TestMirrorHook_*` tests. |
| metrics-extraction bead | Implement the `.ddx/metrics/attempts.jsonl` writer. Define the minimum row schema. Emit one row per finalized attempt. Add `TestMetricsExtraction_*` tests. |
| analyze CLI bead | Implement `ddx metrics` (or `ddx runs analyze`) query commands over `attempts.jsonl`. |

All implementation beads anchor against this ADR (`spec:ADR-026`). Any change to
the storage tier boundaries, retention behavior, hook contract, or metrics schema
requires an ADR-026 amendment in the same PR as the code change.
